title: 2011 St Helens R.F.C. season
wikitext:
{{Use dmy dates|date=April 2023}} {{Infobox rugby league team season | year = 2011 | team_colour = #ffffff | font_colour = #f00823 | league = Super League | league_link = [[Super League XIV]] | league_rank = ''3rd'' | league_wins = 8 | league_draws = 2 | league_losses = 3 | points_for = 376 | points_against = 250 | playoff_result = - | cup = Challenge Cup | cup_result = ''Fourth round'' | cup_wins = - | cup_losses = - | CEO_title = Chairman | CEO = Eamonn McManus | coach_title = Head coach | coach = [[Royce Simmons]] | captain = [[Paul Wellens]] and
[[James Graham (rugby league)|James Graham]] | stadium = [[Stobart Stadium]] | avg_attendance = - | high_attendance = - | top_tries = [[Jamie Foster]] (25) | top_goals = [[Jamie Foster]] (151) | top_points = [[Jamie Foster]] (402) | prev_season = [[2010 St Helens RLFC season|2010]] | next_season = [[2012 St Helens RLFC season|2012]] | list = yes | list_link = List of St Helens RLFC seasons | homejersey = St. Helens home jersey 2010.svg | awayjersey = }}The '''2011 [[St Helens R.F.C.]] season''' was the club's 137th in their history; their 116th in [[rugby league]].{{cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/club.php?id=33498|title=St Helens RLFC Club Profile|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=15 July 2009|access-date=2 November 2010|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100921105101/http://www.superleague.co.uk/club.php?id=33498|archive-date=21 September 2010|url-status=dead}} They were in contention for the [[Super League XIV|Super League]] title (with this also being their 16th consecutive year in Super League) as well as the 110th edition of the [[Challenge Cup]] and, as announced in May 2011, the new [[Rugby league nines|Rugby League 9s]] tournament, to take place across June.{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/page/936|title=Saints to Take on Nines|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=23 May 2011|access-date=23 May 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120313214106/http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/page/936|archive-date=13 March 2012|url-status=dead}} The 2011 season was also the clubs' first since unofficially rebranding as '''Saints RL''', following on from their move away from their home of 120 years, [[Knowsley Road]], to relocate to [[Widnes]], [[Cheshire]] and the [[Halton Stadium]], which they shared with [[Widnes Vikings]]. This was Australian [[Royce Simmons]] first year as head coach of the club, taking over from fellow Australian [[Mick Potter]], who had two years in charge.{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/2698|title=Saints Appoint Royce Simmons|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=22 July 2010|access-date=2 November 2010}}{{cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12206_6274263,00.html |title=Simmons to coach Saints |publisher=Sky Sports |date=22 July 2010 |access-date=2 November 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100923185532/http://www.skysports.com/story/0%2C19528%2C12206_6274263%2C00.html |archive-date=23 September 2010 |url-status=live }} The position of club captain at St Helens was shared by [[Paul Wellens]] and [[James Graham (rugby league)|James Graham]],{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/2980|title=Duo Named As Captains For 2011|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=1 February 2011|access-date=2011-02-03}}{{cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=20878|title=Saints name Wellens and Graham joint captains|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=1 February 2011|access-date=2011-02-03}}{{cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12206_6716940,00.html|title=Saints duo named captains|publisher=Sky Sports|date=1 February 2011|access-date=2011-02-03}} following the retirement of club legend [[Keiron Cunningham]] at the end of the [[2010 St Helens RLFC season|2010 season]]. They began their season by taking on bitter rivals [[Wigan Warriors]] at the [[Magic Weekend]], in a rerun of their defeat at the [[2010 Super League Grand Final]],{{cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/rugbyleague/match_report/0,19936,11070_3326090,00.html |title=Warriors too good for Saints |publisher=Sky Sports |date=2 October 2010 |access-date=2 November 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101005001030/http://www.skysports.com/rugbyleague/match_report/0%2C19936%2C11070_3326090%2C00.html |archive-date=5 October 2010 |url-status=dead }} a game that ended in a 16–16 draw.{{cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=21003|title=St Helens 16-16 Wigan Warriors|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=12 February 2011|access-date=12 February 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120905094216/http://www.superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=21003|archive-date=5 September 2012|url-status=dead}} ==Pre-season== St Helens ended 2010 with yet another Grand Final defeat, their fourth in a row, to bitter rivals Wigan. It was evident that the personnel at the club, combined with the suitability of Mick Potter's position had become surplus to requirements at St Helens, and widespread changes took place. Potter announced mid-2010 that he would be leaving for [[Bradford Bulls]],{{cite web|url=http://superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=18413 |title=Potter lands Bradford Bulls job |publisher=superleague.co.uk |date=16 July 2010 |access-date=27 November 2010 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120120132833/http://superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=18413 |archive-date=20 January 2012 }}{{cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12206_6423686,00.html|title=No magical ending for Potter|publisher=Sky Sports|date=2 October 2010|access-date=27 November 2010}} his replacement, Royce Simmons; someone who had worked under [[Tim Sheens]] for many years at [[Wests Tigers]] and with the [[Australia national rugby league team|Australian national side]]. The very fact that St Helens had been, in many fans' eyes 'reduced' to looking for assistant coaches in the [[National Rugby League|NRL]] drew a little bit of criticism, but overall, the feeling was positive over Simmons' appointment.{{cite news|publisher=St Helens RLFC|title=Simmons proud to be at Saints |author=St Helens RLFC|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/2914|access-date=27 November 2010|date=18 November 2010| archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20101129043327/http://saintsrlfc.com/news/2914| archive-date= 29 November 2010 | url-status= live}}{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2010/jun/28/rugby-league-st-helens-coach|title=Royce Simmons the surprising front-runner to take over at St Helens|publisher=guardian.co.uk|date=28 June 2010|access-date=27 November 2010 | location=London | first=Andy | last=Wilson}} The coach would not be the only change either. Club legend Keiron Cunningham retired from playing at the age of 33, after 16 years of paying first team rugby for St Helens, with centre [[Matt Gidley]] doing likewise, and returning to Australia. A number of others were to leave too; a combination of age and not being good enough for squad selection under Simmons costing the St Helens careers of 12 of the 2010 squad, most notably, arguably, [[Maurie Fa'asavalu]], who switched codes with [[Harlequin F.C.|Harlequins]]. The influx was far fewer than the outgoing numbers during the transfer period, with only three names coming into the squad for 2011 from other clubs; these being prop [[Josh Perry]],{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/2616|title=Saints Sign Josh Perry|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=15 June 2010|access-date=27 November 2010}} [[England national rugby league team|England]] centre [[Michael Shenton]],{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/2722|title=Saints Sign Michael Shenton|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=3 August 2010|access-date=27 November 2010}} and fellow England cap and prop [[Louie McCarthy-Scarsbrook]],{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/2796|title=Saints Sign Louie McCarthy-Scarsbrook|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=3 September 2010|access-date=27 November 2010}} from [[Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles]], [[Castleford Tigers]] and [[London Broncos#2006–2011: Harlequins Rugby League|Harlequins]] respectively. There were also four new faces in the first team by means of the reserve and academy system at St Helens. Pre-season fixtures announced included a game against [[Huddersfield Giants]] at the Halton Stadium on 29 January and the Karalius Cup game against Widnes, again at the Stobart, on the 23rd. ==Sponsors== St Helens are sponsored in the main by Medicash, after previously being sponsored by both Frontline and the Liverpool-based health care company on the home and away jerseys respectively. However, Medicash took over compete sponsorship of both the away and home strips for 2011. The jerseys themselves will are manufactured by [[International Sports Clothing|ISC]], after 7 years with [[Puma AG|Puma]]. St Helens also welcomed Alexandra Business Park and Ruskin Leisure Park as partners for the 2011 season. Below is a list of St Helens sponsors and partners for 2011: {| class="wikitable" |- ! colspan="2" |2011 St Helens RLFC sponsors |- !width=28%|Capacity !width=22%|Name |- |Kit manufacturer |[[International Sports Clothing|ISC]] |- |Stadium sponsors |[[Stobart]] |- |Home jersey front sponsor |[[Medicash]] |- |Shorts sponsor |[[Tudor North West]] |- |Back panel sponsor | [[Hattons Solicitors]] |- |Collar sponsor |[[Live'smart]] |- |Front panel sponsors |[[Alexandra Business Park]] |- |Arm sponsor |[[James Edward Land Rover]] |- |Arm sponsor |[[Knowsley Safari Park]] |- |Overseas travel partners |[[Emirates Airline|Emirates]] |- |Travel partners |[[Arriva]] |- |Travel partners |[[Hattons Travel]] |- |Website partners |{{usurped|1=[https://web.archive.org/web/20010202031200/http://www.ems-internet.co.uk/ EMS Internet]}} |- |Stadium partners |[[Science in Sport]] |- |Stadium partners |[[Macmillan Cancer Support]] |- |Stadium partners |[[Playing for Success]] |- |Stadium partners |[[Ruskin Leisure Limited]] |- |Ball providers |[[Steeden]] |- |League partners |[[engage Mutual]] |} ==2011 transfers== ===In=== [[Image:Louie McCarthy-Scarsbrook Quins.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Louie McCarthy-Scarsbrook in action for Harlequins in 2010]] {| class="wikitable" style="width:65%; font-size:90%;" |- ! colspan=11 | In |- ! When left |- |[[Kyle Eastmond]] || Code switch ([[Bath Rugby]]) || November 2011 |- |[[James Graham (rugby league)|James Graham]] || [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]] || November 2011 |} ==Fixtures and results== ===Friendlies=== ====Karalius Cup==== [[Widnes Vikings]] 20-52 '''St Helens''' {{Rugbyleaguebox | bg = #eeeeee | team1 = [[Widnes Vikings]] {{leagueicon|Widnes|16}} | team2 = {{leagueicon|St Helens|16}} [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] | score = 20–52 | report = [http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/2963 (Report)]| points1 = Tries: [[Shane Grady|Grady]],

[[Gary Wheeler (rugby league)|Wheeler]] Goals: Wheeler (0/2)
[[Jamie Foster|Foster]] (0/1) |points2 = Tries: [[David Hodgson (rugby league)|Hodgson]],
[[Kevin Brown (English rugby league)|Brown]],
[[Danny Brough|Brough]],
[[Eorl Crabtree|Crabtree]],
[[Shaun Lunt|Lunt]],
[[Michael Lawrence (rugby league)|Lawrence]] Goals: Brough (6/6) | date = Monday 7 February 2011{{cite web|url=http://saintsrlfc.com/results/match-report/369|title=Saints v Huddersfield Giants|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=7 February 2011|access-date=7 February 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518101035/http://www.saintsrlfc.com/results/match-report/369|archive-date=18 May 2011|url-status=dead}} | stadium = [[Halton Stadium]], [[Widnes]] | attendance = 1,258 | referee = [[Steve Ganson]] | }} ===[[Super League XVI]]=== ====[[Magic Weekend]]==== '''St Helens''' 16-16 [[Wigan Warriors]] St Helens produced a characteristic fightback in the last 15 minutes to snatch an unlikely draw against a well-drilled, energetic Wigan side. After tries from Ryan Hoffman, Harrison Hansen and George Carmont combined with two disallowed Saints efforts from [[James Roby]] and [[Michael Shenton]], the game looked dead and buried at 16–0 to Wigan; with the auld enemy on top in terms of field position, defense, kicking and handling. However, Saints forged a comeback through a rare missed tackle from an otherwise outstanding Wigan defense when Roby put Jon Wilkin away under the posts, bettered by the boot of Jamie Foster. The energy levels visibly went up, and Tony Puletua powered through the Wigan line and somehow grounded the ball with a mass of bodies surrounding him, again Foster good with the goal. It took Saints another five minutes-a period in which Wigan looked threatening once more-to get their draw, with a forward-looking pass from [[Jonny Lomax]] finding Francis Meli in space out on the touchline, Foster about three yards wide with what would have been the game clinching kick. Wigan's Sam Tomkins and Saints scrum-half [[Kyle Eastmond]] exchanged failed drop-goal attempts late on as both sides settled on a point each in the game of the day at the Magic Weekend. {{Rugbyleaguebox | bg = #eeeeee | team1 = [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] {{leagueicon|St Helens|16}} | team2 = {{leagueicon|Wigan|16}} [[Wigan Warriors]] | score = 16–16 | report = [http://www.superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=21003 (Report)]| points1 = Tries: [[Jon Wilkin|Wilkin]],
[[Tony Puletua|Puletua]],
[[Francis Meli|Meli]] Goals: [[Jamie Foster|Foster]] (2/3) |points2 = Tries: [[Ryan Hoffman|Hoffman]],
[[Harrison Hansen|Hansen]],
[[George Carmont|Carmont]] Goals: [[Paul Deacon|Deacon]] (1/1), [[Sam Tomkins|S. Tomkins]] (1/2) | date = Saturday 12 February 2011 | stadium = [[Millennium Stadium]], [[Cardiff]] | attendance = 30,891 | referee = [[Richard Silverwood]] | }} ====Round 2==== [[Salford Red Devils|Salford City Reds]] 22-56 '''St Helens''' St Helens created for the first time this season their most attractive brand of football; free-flowing and point scoring. However, the job was done after an hour, and they seemed to take their foot of the gas-a trait they may not wish to fall into permanently. Saints started sharply against an out of sorts Salford side coming off the back of a heavy defeat to Crusaders the week previous, with tries from Chris Flannery and the first of two from Kyle Eastmond. Salford hit back through Luke Patten, but it took them to the second half to post more points; a spell in which St Helens totally dominated and scored another five tries of their own. James Roby in a brilliant performance, Francis Meli, Jonny Lomax and Ade Gardner all crossed to give Saints a 38–6 lead at the break. The second half started in bizarre fashion, with two tries in the first minute from either side; for Salford Ashley Gibson, for St Helens, Lee Gaskell. Paul Clough and Michael Shenton completed the scoring for Saints, before Stefan Ratchford and Jodie Broughton made the scoreline a little more respectable for the Reds. {{Rugbyleaguebox | bg = #eeeeee | team1 = [[Salford Red Devils|Salford City Reds]] {{leagueicon|Salford City Reds|16}} | team2 = {{leagueicon|St Helens|16}} [[St Helens R.F.C.]] | score = 22–56 | report = [http://www.skysports.com/rugbyleague/match_report/0,19936,11070_3343349,00.html (Report)]| points1 = Tries: [[Luke Patten|Patten]],
[[Ashley Gibson|Gibson]],
[[Stefan Ratchford|Ratchford]],
[[Jodie Broughton|Broughton]] Goals: [[Daniel Holdsworth (rugby league)|Holdsworth]] (3/4) |points2 = Tries: [[Chris Flannery (rugby league)|Flannery]],
[[Kyle Eastmond|Eastmond]] (2),
[[James Roby|Roby]],
[[Francis Meli|Meli]],
[[Jonny Lomax|Lomax]],
[[Ade Gardner|Gardner]],
[[Lee Gaskell|Gaskell]],
[[Paul Clough|Clough]],
[[Michael Shenton|Shenton]] Goals: [[Jamie Foster|Foster]] (8/10) | date = Friday 18 February 2011{{cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/rugbyleague/match_report/0,19936,11070_3343349,00.html|title=Saints rip Reds to shreds|publisher=Sky Sports|date=18 February 2011|access-date=18 February 2011}} | stadium = [[The Willows, Salford]] | attendance = 5,929 | referee = [[Robert Hicks (referee)|Robert Hicks]] | }} ====Round 3==== '''St Helens''' 18-25 [[Warrington Wolves]] For the first time in 22 games, Warrington won against St Helens in Super League on a miserable night, both meteorically and psychologically for St Helens, at the Stobart Stadium. It was also Warrington's first ever win in Super League away from home, and was never really in doubt from the moment Lee Briers crossed in the second minute, a score which was bettered by Richie Myler and another from the stand-off before half-time, all of which were converted by Brett Hodgson to give the Wolves an 18-0 half time advantage. Warrington continued to pile on the pressure against an out of sorts Saints side, when Louis Anderson finished from a Lee Gaskell error, Hodgson again with the goal for 24–0. With around ten minutes to play, Michael Shenton crossed for Saints to give them the slightest glimmer of hope; a hope crushed by a Briers field goal that effectively meant Saints had to score three times. Chris Flannery and Lee Gaskell added late scores to give the score an account of respectability, but the performance showed that plenty of defensive work needs to be done for Saints' trophy charge this season. {{Rugbyleaguebox | bg = #eeeeee | team1 = [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] {{leagueicon|St Helens|16}} | team2 = {{leagueicon|Warrington|16}} [[Warrington Wolves]] | score = 18–25 | report = [http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=5961 (Report)]| points1 = Tries: [[Michael Shenton|Shenton]],
[[Chris Flannery (rugby league)|Flannery]],
[[Lee Gaskell|Gaskell]] Goals: [[Jonny Lomax|Lomax]] (1/1),
[[Jamie Foster|Foster]] (2/2) |points2 = Tries: [[Lee Briers|Briers]] (2),
[[Richard Myler|Myler]],
[[Louis Anderson|Anderson]] Goals: [[Brett Hodgson|Hodgson]] (4/4)
Drop Goals: Briers| date = Friday 26 February 2011{{cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=5961|title=St Helens 18-25 Warrington Wolves|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=26 February 2011|access-date=10 March 2011}} | stadium = [[Halton Stadium]], [[Widnes]] | attendance = 10,514 | referee = [[Phil Bentham]] | }} ====Round 4==== [[Catalans Dragons]] 16-22 '''St Helens''' Bouncing back from their first defeat at home to Warrington in Super League, St Helens produced the defensive grit that was all too absent the week previous against a big Dragons pack. Saints opened the scoring with a free-flowing move finished off by Chris Flannery, bettered by the boot of Kyle Eastmond. Despite massive Catalans' pressure, five minutes later Ade Gardner found the line at the corner after good work by Eastmond, Paul Wellens and Michael Shenton, Eastmond this time wide with the kick at goal. The Dragons pressure then duly paid off, with Setaimata Sa latching on to a Scott Dureau kick, Dureau missing with the conversion but Saints nudged further in front with James Graham getting his first of the season, backing Wellens up well to dot down under the posts, Eastmond with a simple conversion. After the break, the free flowing football Saints seemed to be clicking with for arguably the first time this season continued with Michael Shenton scoring in the right-hand corner, Eastmond again missing with the goal kick. Catalans brought the game back within touching distance with a Steve Menzies try and Dureau goal, but Saints edged further in front with an Eastmond penalty, followed by a consolation try and goal from Dureau that made the final score 22–16 to Saints. {{Rugbyleaguebox | bg = #eeeeee | team1 = [[Catalans Dragons]] {{leagueicon|Catalans|16}} | team2 = {{leagueicon|St Helens|16}} [[St Helens R.F.C.]] | score = 16–22 | report = [http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=5970 (Report)]| points1 = Tries: [[Setaimata Sa|Sa]],
[[Steve Menzies|Menzies]],
[[Scott Dureau|Dureau]] Goals: Dureau (2/3) |points2 = Tries: [[Chris Flannery (rugby league)|Flannery]],
[[James Graham (rugby league)|Graham]],
[[Ade Gardner|Gardner]],
[[Michael Shenton|Shenton]] Goals: [[Kyle Eastmond|Eastmond]] (3/5) | date = Saturday 5 March 2011{{cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=5970|title=Catalans 16-22 St Helens|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=5 March 2011|access-date=5 March 2011}} | stadium = [[Stade Gilbert Brutus]], [[Perpignan]], France | attendance = 7,095 | referee = [[Thierry Alibert]] | }} ====Round 5==== '''St Helens''' 16-27 [[London Broncos#2006–2011: Harlequins Rugby League|Harlequins]] St Helens were simply out classed, out enthused and out played by a flying Quins side, who produced a first half of rugby similar to the days of Saints dominance in the late 1990s/early 2000s. Racing into a 20–0 lead at the break thanks to tries from Luke Dorn, Karl Pryce and Tony Clubb, with Luke Gale adding four goals, Saints were never in the contest, producing mistakes and at best very flat rugby, compared to the very lively Quins. It took until the 48th minute for Saints to break their duck, with Francis Meli latching onto a cut-out ball from [[Lee Gaskell]]. An unlikely, and in truth undeserved comeback was on the cards when captain James Graham powered over, with Kyle Eastmond converting to bring Saints back within two scores. However these hopes were soon dashed when, from the kick-off, Nick Kouparitsas intercepted a James Roby pass; a feat characteristic of the sloppiness of St Helens and the sharpness of the Londoners continuous throughout the game. Five minutes from time, Gale added a drop-goal to wrap up the points before Roby grabbed a last minute consolation after an offload from [[Tony Puletua]]. Either the unpredictability of the competition, or the erratic form of the Saints was to blame here; only time would tell as the competition progressed. {{Rugbyleaguebox | bg = #eeeeee | team1 = [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] {{leagueicon|St Helens|16}} | team2 = {{leagueicon|Harlequins|16}} [[London Broncos#2006–2011: Harlequins Rugby League|Harlequins]] | score = 16–27 | report = [http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=5976 (Report)]| points1 = Tries: [[Francis Meli|Meli]],
[[James Graham (rugby league)|Graham]],
[[James Roby|Roby]] Goals: [[Kyle Eastmond|Eastmond]] (1/2),
[[Jamie Foster|Foster]] (1/1) |points2 = Tries: [[Luke Dorn|Dorn]],
[[Karl Pryce|Pryce]],
[[Tony Clubb|Clubb]],
[[Nick Kouparitsas|Kouparitsas]] Goals: [[Luke Gale|Gale]] (5/5)
Drop Goals: Gale| date = Friday 11 March 2011{{cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=5976|title=St Helens 16-27 Harlequins|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=11 March 2011|access-date=23 March 2011}} | stadium = [[Halton Stadium]], [[Widnes]] | attendance = 6,050 | referee = [[Gareth Hewer]] | }} ====Round 6==== [[Leeds Rhinos]] 16–30 '''St Helens''' The unpredictability of Saints this season was once again on show as they beat a Leeds side in a similar man-power situation to Saints. Added to the significance of the win was that scrum-half [[Kyle Eastmond]] was internally suspended for a serious breach of club discipline,{{cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12206_6816430,00.html|title=Saints suspend Eastmond|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=16 March 2011|access-date=23 March 2011}} so inexperienced halves [[Lee Gaskell]] and [[Jonny Lomax]] had to step up to the fore. The game started in ferocious style, both defences repelling each other, before Jamie Foster squeezed in at the left corner and converted his own try to give Saints the lead on 27 minutes. Leeds hit back five minutes before the break when Ryan hall scored, Kevin Sinfield kicking the goal for 6–6 at the break. It was Leeds who broke the second half deadlock with a very controversial try. The ball from [[Kallum Watkins]] to eventual scorer Zak Hardaker appeared to go yards forward, but, after reference to the video referee, the try, which came about form fine handling from Hardaker, was awarded. This decision only spurred Saints on, and, after a towering [[Bomb (kick)|bomb]] from Gaskell, Michael Shenton dotted down, Foster again kicking the two for 12–10. The lead again switched hands when Paul McShane nudged over from dummy-half, Sinfield making it 16–12, but St Helens managed to find something that had seemingly escaped them hitherto; heart. Francis Meli barged past Watkins after a good ball from [[Scott Moore (rugby league)|Scott Moore]], before Paul Wellens timed his running line to perfection to latch onto a Gaskell pass. Foster converted both for 24–16. At this point the points were rapped up, despite Leeds' best efforts in reply. But the best, most exhilarating piece of football was still to come, when Meli flicked the ball out to the left wing, Foster caught the ball behind his back, switched it between his hands to score in the tightest of circumstances. To make matters better and put the proverbial icing on the cake of victory for Saints, Foster maintained his perfect kicking display with another goal from the touchline. {{Rugbyleaguebox | bg = #eeeeee | team1 = [[Leeds Rhinos]] {{leagueicon|Leeds|16}} | team2 = {{leagueicon|St Helens|16}} [[St Helens R.F.C.]] | score = 16–30 | report = [http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=6385 (Report)]| points1 = Tries: [[Ryan Hall (rugby league)|Hall]],
[[Zak Hardaker|Hardaker]],
[[Paul McShane (rugby league)|McShane]] Goals: [[Kevin Sinfield|Sinfield]] (2/3) |points2 = Tries: [[Jamie Foster|Foster]] (2),
[[Michael Shenton|Shenton]],
[[Francis Meli|Meli]],
[[Paul Wellens|Wellens]] Goals: [[Jamie Foster|Foster]] (5/5) | date = Saturday 19 March 2011{{cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=6385|title=Leeds 16-30 St Helens|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=19 March 2011|access-date=23 March 2011}} | stadium = [[Headingley Rugby Stadium|Headingley]], [[Leeds]] | attendance = 16,034 | referee = [[Richard Silverwood]] | }} ====Round 7==== '''St Helens''' 28-16 [[Bradford Bulls]] For the first time in the season, St Helens showed some signs of consistency, with a comfortable win over a plucky Bradford outfit. It was the Bulls who made the better start though, with Shaun Ainscough latching onto a [[Marc Herbert]] kick, Patrick Ah van converting. Saints hit back in controversial fashion, with Paul Wellens' forward-looking pass falling into the arms of Jamie Foster, who squeezed in at the left-hand corner. Foster missed the goal, but made no mistake after Wellens crashed over. Three tries shared between Wellens, Chris Flannery and Foster in a blistering spell in which [[Paul Sykes (rugby league)|Paul Sykes]] was sin-binned effectively sealed the result, before Ah Van and Ainscough grabbed consolation efforts for the Bulls when Saints were down to 11 men through yellow cards. {{Rugbyleaguebox | bg = #eeeeee | team1 = [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] {{leagueicon|St Helens|16}} | team2 = {{leagueicon|Bradford|16}} [[Bradford Bulls]] | score = 28–16 | report = [http://www.saintsrlfc.com/results/match-report/524 (Report)]| points1 = Tries: [[Jamie Foster|Foster]] (2),
[[Paul Wellens|Wellens]] (2),
[[Chris Flannery (rugby league)|Flannery]] Goals: Foster (4/6) |points2 = Tries: [[Shaun Ainscough|Ainscough]] (2),
[[Patrick Ah Van|Ah Van]] Goals: Ah Van (2/3) |date = 25 March 2011{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/results/match-report/524|title=St Helens 28-16 Bradford|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=25 March 2011|access-date=4 April 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518100501/http://www.saintsrlfc.com/results/match-report/524|archive-date=18 May 2011|url-status=dead}} | stadium = [[Halton Stadium]], [[Widnes]] | attendance = 7,676 | referee = [[Richard Silverwood]] | }} ====Round 8==== '''St Helens''' 34-16 [[Hull Kingston Rovers]] Saints extended their winning run to three games with a routine win over Hull KR. It was the Rovers who started the better though, with Kris Welham scoring on 10 minutes. However, the Saints hit back in typical fashion, Sia Soliola getting his first try for the club. In similar style, Louie McCarthy-Scarsbrook scored his first for the club since his winter move from Harlequins, before scoring an almost carbon copy of his first minutes later, with Jamie Foster's boot giving St Helens an 18–6 advantage. And [[Ade Gardner]]'s break gave Paul Wellens the opportunity to scorch in unopposed; 24–6 at half time. Foster himself, so often the points machine with the boot, got on the try list with his fifth in three games, missing the conversion. On his 200th appearance, James Graham crashed over, before consolation efforts from the Robins from Welham and Jake Webster gave the scoreline a somewhat undeserved credibility. {{Rugbyleaguebox | bg = #eeeeee | team1 = [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] {{leagueicon|St Helens|16}} | team2 = {{leagueicon|Hull KR|16}} [[Hull Kingston Rovers]] | score = 34–16 | report = [http://www.saintsrlfc.com/results/match-report/152 (Report)]| points1 = Tries: [[Iosia Soliola|Soliola]],
[[Louie McCarthy-Scarsbrook|McCarthy-Scarsbrook]] (2),
[[Paul Wellens|Wellens]],
[[Jamie Foster|Foster]],
[[James Graham (rugby league)|Graham]] Goals: Foster (5/6) |points2 = Tries: [[Kris Welham|Welham]] (2),
[[Jake Webster|Webster]] Goals: [[Craig Hall (rugby league born 1988)|Hall]] (2/3) |date = 1 April 2011{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/results/match-report/152|title=St Helens 34-16 Hull KR|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=1 April 2011|access-date=4 April 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518101218/http://www.saintsrlfc.com/results/match-report/152|archive-date=18 May 2011|url-status=dead}} | stadium = [[Halton Stadium]], [[Widnes]] | attendance = 7,740 | referee = [[Ben Thaler]] | }} ====Round 9==== [[Crusaders Rugby League|Crusaders]] 18-34 '''St Helens''' Although St Helens' fantastic form continued in this win over the 'Cru', Saints were guilty of taking their foot off the gas after half-time, and not capitalising on a scrappy display for the Welsh outfit in the second period. Young half-back combination Jonny Lomax and Lee Gaskell, assisted by the on-fire James Roby sent Saints on their way, with Jamie Foster's kicking giving them a 12–0 lead early on. After a brief spell of Crusaders pressure, Paul Wellens took a grubber from Gaskell to score Saints' third of the evening, before Gareth Thomas gave Crusaders a bit of hope. However, Saints wrapped up the win before half time, with two further tries, Roby himself and Ade Gardner crossing to give St Helens a 30–6 lead at the break. The impressive Tony Martin and Lloyd White gave Crusaders a sight at a fightback but Gardner's second on the full-time siren ensured the win for Saints. {{Rugbyleaguebox | bg = #eeeeee | team1 = [[Crusaders Rugby League]] {{leagueicon|Crusaders|16}} | team2 = {{leagueicon|St Helens|16}} [[St Helens R.F.C.]] | score = 18–34 | report = [http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=6002 (Report)]| points1 = Tries: [[Gareth Thomas (rugby, born 1974)|Thomas]],
[[Tony Martin (rugby league)|Martin]],
[[Lloyd White (rugby league)|White]] Goals: [[Clinton Schifcofske|Schifcofske]] (1/1),
[[Michael Witt|Witt]] (2/2) |points2 = Tries: [[Jonny Lomax|Lomax]],
[[Lee Gaskell|Gaskell]],
[[Paul Wellens|Wellens]],
[[James Roby|Roby]],
[[Ade Gardner|Gardner]] (2) Goals: [[Jamie Foster|Foster]] (5/6) | date = Friday 8 April 2011{{cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=6002|title=Crusaders 18-34 St Helens|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=8 April 2011|access-date=30 April 2011}} | stadium = [[Racecourse Ground]], [[Wrexham]] | attendance = 4,002 | referee = [[Robert Hicks (referee)|Robert Hicks]] | }} ====Round 10==== '''St Helens''' 52-6 [[Wakefield Trinity|Wakefield Trinity Wildcats]] St Helens produced their most dominant game of the season, with a resounding 9-try-to-1 win over Wakefield. The game started off in tight fashion, with Saints' pressure being cancelled out by some rugged if plucky Wildcats defence. However, the deadlock was broken on 17 minutes, with Paul Wellens' long ball finding the in-form Jamie Foster out wide who crossed and scored a goal. Jonny Lomax jinked his way through before [[Jon Wilkin]] found a gaping hole in the Wakefield defence to set up Foster, who cantered over for his second, and the floodgates were well and truly open. Michael Shenton crossed before half time, before Chris Dean, a former Saints player sped 60 meters on an intercept to give Wakefield a glimmer of hope. However, Ade Gardner went over 7 minutes later, and tries from Gary Wheeler, Wellens, Tony Puletua and Tom Makinson completed the rout. {{Rugbyleaguebox | bg = #eeeeee | team1 = [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] {{leagueicon|St Helens|16}} | team2 = {{leagueicon|Wakefield|16}} [[Wakefield Trinity|Wakefield Trinity Wildcats]] | score = 52–6 | report = [http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=6011 (Report)]| points1 = Tries: [[Jamie Foster|Foster]] (2),
[[Jonny Lomax|Lomax]],
[[Michael Shenton|Shenton]],
[[Ade Gardner|Gardner]],
[[Gary Wheeler (rugby league)|Wheeler]],
[[Paul Wellens|Wellens]],
[[Tony Puletua|Puletua]],
[[Thomas Makinson|Makinson]] Goals: Foster (8/9) |points2 = Tries: [[Chris Dean (rugby league)|Dean]] Goals: [[Josh Griffin|Griffin]] (1/1) |date = 15 April 2011{{cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=6011|title=St Helens 52-6 Wakefield|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=15 April 2011|access-date=30 April 2011}} | stadium = [[Halton Stadium]], [[Widnes]] | attendance = 7,003 | referee = [[Steve Ganson]] | }} ====Round 11==== [[Wigan Warriors]] 28-24 '''St Helens''' St Helens' run came to an end in somewhat unfair circumstances after a classic with rivals Wigan. An injury ravaged Saints led 10-0 after 35 minutes through Michael Shenton and Jamie Foster scores. But Wigan hit back close to half-time, with Josh Charnley crossing in the right corner. Tries from Pat Richards (2) and another from Charnley put Wigan ahead for the first time in the game as they sought to dominate. And at 22–10, a youthful Saints side could have been forgiven for giving up, but in typical fashion, they hit back quickly, with Foster's second and Tom Makinson getting his second try in as many games dragging them back on terms. And Saints looked to have won the game, with Foster's boot from a thirty-yard penalty giving them a 24–22 lead with 7 minutes to play. However, Wigan won it through a last minute try from Liam Farrell, after a great delayed pass from [[Paul Deacon]]. An undeserved defeat, but a heartening display gave the game some credibility from Saints' perspective. {{Rugbyleaguebox | bg = #eeeeee | team1 = [[Wigan Warriors]] {{leagueicon|Wigan|16}} | team2 = {{leagueicon|St Helens|16}} [[St Helens R.F.C.]] | score = 28–24 | report = [http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=6020 (Report)]| points1 = Tries: [[Josh Charnley|Charnley]] (2),

[[Jamie Foster|Foster]],
[[Thomas Makinson|Makinson]] Goals: Foster (4/5) | date = Friday 22 April 2011{{cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=6020|title=Wigan 28-24 St Helens|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=22 April 2011|access-date=30 April 2011}} | stadium = [[DW Stadium]], [[Wigan]] | attendance = 24,057 | referee = [[Richard Silverwood]] | }} ====Round 12==== '''St Helens''' 22-20 [[Castleford Tigers]] {{Rugbyleaguebox | bg = #eeeeee | team1 = [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] {{leagueicon|St Helens|16}} | team2 = {{leagueicon|Castleford|16}} [[Castleford Tigers]] | score = 22–20 | report = [http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=6026 (Report)]| points1 = Tries: [[Tom Armstrong (rugby league)|Armstrong]] (2),
